questions.question: Explain the difference between vertex coloring and face coloring in a colored cube.
keyPoints: Vertex coloring assigns colors to individual vertices and interpolates across faces
Face coloring assigns a single uniform color per face
Vertex coloring produces gradient effects while face coloring produces solid colors

questions.question: What is back-face culling and why is it important for performance?
keyPoints: Back-face culling discards polygons facing away from the camera
It reduces the number of polygons to be processed and rendered
This optimization speeds up rendering pipeline significantly

questions.question: List and explain the three main coordinate spaces in 3D graphics rendering.
keyPoints: Model space: coordinates relative to object's center
World space: coordinates in the global scene after model transformation
Screen/NDC space: final 2D coordinates after projection

questions.question: Describe the rendering pipeline stages for a colored cube.
keyPoints: Vertex processing: apply transformations to vertices
Primitive assembly: form triangles from vertices
Clipping: remove geometry outside view frustum
Rasterization: convert to pixels
Fragment processing: apply coloring and shading

questions.question: What is double buffering and how does it prevent screen flicker?
keyPoints: Double buffering uses two display buffers (front and back)
Rendering happens on the back buffer while front buffer displays
Buffers are swapped after rendering completes, preventing partial updates visible to user

questions.question: Explain the role of transformation matrices in modeling a colored cube.
keyPoints: Transformation matrices (4x4) perform rotation, scaling, translation
Model matrix transforms object from local to world space
Multiple transformations can be combined into a single matrix

questions.question: Explain the complete process of modeling and rendering a colored cube in OpenGL with proper vertex specification, color assignment, and transformation pipeline.
keyPoints: Define 8 vertices with (x,y,z) coordinates for cube corners
Specify 6 faces using vertex indices with proper winding order
Assign colors per vertex or per face for coloring
Apply model transformation (rotation, translation, scaling)
Apply view transformation (camera positioning)
Apply projection transformation (perspective)
Perform clipping and rasterization
Apply fragment operations including depth testing
Final display via double buffering

questions.question: Compare flat shading, Gouraud shading, and Phong shading techniques for a colored cube. Explain which provides the best visual quality and why.
keyPoints: Flat shading: calculates lighting once per face, uniform color per face, faceted look
Gouraud shading: interpolates vertex colors across face, smooth but limited
Phong shading: interpolates normals per pixel, most realistic highlights
Trade-offs: flat is fastest, phong is highest quality but computationally expensive
For cube: flat shows geometric edges clearly, others may obscure cube nature

questions.question: Derive and explain how the depth buffer (Z-buffer) algorithm resolves visibility for overlapping colored cubes in a 3D scene.
keyPoints: Depth buffer stores depth (Z) value for each pixel
Initialize all pixels to maximum depth (far plane)
When rendering, compare new fragment depth with stored depth
If new depth < stored depth, replace color and depth buffer
After processing all objects, color buffer contains correctly visible surfaces
Z-buffer handles complex scenes with arbitrary geometry
Formula: Znew < Zstored ? write : discard

questions.question: Design a mathematical model for a colored cube including vertex coordinates, face definitions, normal vectors, and transformation matrices to rotate it 45 degrees around the Y-axis.
keyPoints: 8 vertices at (±1,±1,±1) forming unit cube
Faces defined by 4 vertices each with CCW winding
Normal vectors perpendicular to each face (6 normals)
Rotation matrix for 45° Y-axis: [cosθ 0 sinθ 0; 0 1 0 0; -sinθ 0 cosθ 0; 0 0 0 1]
Apply matrix to all vertices: V' = R × V
Homogeneous coordinates use 4th element as 1
